title: Links & Navigation
summary: Learn how to connect web pages together using anchor tags, build in-page navigation with IDs, and create a clean, accessible nav bar.

blocks.md: The web is literally a *web* — billions of pages linked together. Every clickable link you have ever followed was created with one tiny HTML element: `<a>`. Mastering anchors unlocks the ability to guide your readers from one page to another, jump them to a section of a long document, or open an external resource in a new tab. By the end of this lesson you will understand how links work under the hood and be able to build a real navigation bar.

The `<a>` (anchor) element wraps any content — text, an image, even a button — and makes it a clickable hyperlink. The most important attribute is `href` (short for *hypertext reference*), which tells the browser *where* to go.

blocks.output: Underlined, clickable text that reads "Visit Example".

blocks.title: Links are like street signs
blocks.md: An `<a>` tag is the sign post; the `href` is the address printed on it. Without the address the sign is just a piece of metal — it looks like a link but goes nowhere.

URLs come in two flavors: **absolute** and **relative**.

- An **absolute URL** contains the full address, including the protocol (`https://`), the domain, and the path. Use these when linking to *another website*.
- A **relative URL** describes a path *relative to the current page*. Use these when linking to pages *within your own site* — they automatically adjust when you move your project to a different server.

blocks.code: MDN Web Docs About Us Latest Post
blocks.caption: Use absolute URLs for external sites; use relative URLs within your own project.

blocks.text: Opening Links in a New Tab

blocks.md: Adding `target="_blank"` makes the link open in a new browser tab. This is common for external links so users do not lose their place on your site. However, it comes with a subtle security concern — the new page could access and manipulate the opener page. Always pair `target="_blank"` with `rel="noopener noreferrer"` to close that loophole.

blocks.code: Open in new tab
blocks.output: Clicking the link opens example.com in a new browser tab safely.

blocks.title: Never use target="_blank" without rel="noopener"
blocks.md: Without `rel="noopener noreferrer"`, the newly opened page can call `window.opener` and redirect your original page to a malicious URL — a classic phishing trick. Modern browsers have started patching this, but adding the attribute yourself is still best practice and required by most linters.

Long pages benefit from *jump links* that scroll the reader directly to a section. You do this in two steps:

1. Give the destination element a unique `id` attribute.
2. Set the link's `href` to `#` followed by that id.

When clicked, the browser scrolls smoothly to that element.

blocks.code:
Jump to Contact

Contact Us

Reach us at hello@example.com
blocks.output: Clicking "Jump to Contact" scrolls the page to the Contact section.

blocks.md: A navigation bar is usually a `<nav>` element containing an unordered list (`<ul>`) of links. The `<nav>` landmark helps screen readers and search engines understand that this group of links is site navigation — not just any list.

blocks.code:
• Home
  • About
  • Contact
  • GitHub
blocks.output: A vertical list of four clickable links: Home, About, Contact, GitHub.

blocks.title: Mark the active page
blocks.md: Add `aria-current="page"` to the link that points to the current page. Screen readers announce it, and you can style it with CSS using `[aria-current='page'] { font-weight: bold; }`.

blocks.question: Which link opens a page in a new browser tab AND prevents the opened page from accessing window.opener?
blocks.explanation: You need BOTH target="_blank" (to open a new tab) AND rel="noopener noreferrer" (to block the new page from accessing window.opener). Option A opens a new tab but is insecure; option C is secure but stays in the same tab; option D uses a non-standard target value.

keyTakeaways: The <a href="..."> anchor element is how every hyperlink on the web is made.
Use absolute URLs (with https://) for external sites and relative URLs for pages within your own project.
Always add rel="noopener noreferrer" alongside target="_blank" to prevent a security vulnerability.
Fragment links (#section-id) let users jump to any element on the same page — perfect for long articles.
Wrap your links in a <nav> element to give screen readers and search engines a clear navigation landmark.

exercise.prompt: Build a one-page site skeleton with a navigation bar at the top. The nav should have three links: one to an external site (opens in a new tab, safely), one that jumps to a #features section on the same page, and one relative link to about.html. Below the nav, add a section with id="features" and a short heading inside it.
exercise.starterCode:
<!DOCTYPE html>
<html lang="en">
<head>
  <meta charset="UTF-8">
  <title>My Site</title>
</head>
<body>

  <!-- TODO: Add a <nav> with a <ul> containing three <li> items:
       1. An external link that opens in a new tab (with rel="noopener noreferrer")
       2. A fragment link to #features below
       3. A relative link to about.html
  -->

  <!-- TODO: Add a <section> with id="features" and an <h2> heading inside -->

</body>
</html>
exercise.hint: Remember: target="_blank" needs a partner — rel="noopener noreferrer". For the fragment link, the href should start with #, and the section below must have a matching id attribute.
exercise.solution:
